danger_signals.visual_form: Humanoid sprites emerging from closed doors; shoot projectiles at player
danger_signals.meaning: Active threats present on floor; player must avoid or neutralize

danger_signals.visual_form: Gap in floor where elevator has left or descended; visible drop hazard
danger_signals.meaning: Fall damage hazard; impassable unless elevator present above for jumping

danger_signals.visual_form: Visual/audio cue triggered by time pressure mechanic
danger_signals.meaning: World-state shift: enemy aggression increases, elevator response slows

danger_signals.visual_form: Blackened floor sections with reduced visibility
danger_signals.meaning: Reduced situational awareness; enemies harder to see approaching

danger_signals.visual_form: Collision with enemy projectile, melee attack, or elevator
danger_signals.meaning: Health loss; loss of one life upon hit

safety_signals.visual_form: Elevator car visible in shaft; can be called via controls
safety_signals.meaning: Viable escape or vertical movement option available

safety_signals.visual_form: Floor with closed doors and interior architecture
safety_signals.meaning: Safe traversal space; enemies only emerge from marked doors

safety_signals.visual_form: Lowest level of building with exit car visible
safety_signals.meaning: Victory state when all documents collected; escape available

safety_signals.visual_form: Red door that has been opened and document collected
safety_signals.meaning: Objective progress; one fewer mandatory rooms to complete

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Player sprite; appears at building roof entry point

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Antagonist agents attempting to stop player; armed with firearms
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Emerge from closed doors throughout 30-story building
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Door markers (closed doors are spawn points); become more aggressive under alarm state
Functional architecture; some can be manipulated (lights shot out, elevators called)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Standard enemy patrol behavior; elevators respond normally to input; lights on
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy agents appear at moderate frequency; elevators respond quickly to directional input; visibility clear

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Alarm cue (visual/audio); no specific documented visual change beyond behavior
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy agents become more aggressive and spawn more frequently; elevator response time increases (slower)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Blackened/darkened floor rendering; reduced lighting or near-total darkness
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player vision reduced; enemies harder to anticipate; adds stealth/difficulty element

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Player transported back to highest uncompleted floor
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Forced progression: cannot escape until all red-door documents collected

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Escape path to basement and getaway car becomes active
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Victory condition available; player can navigate to ground floor and exit

environmental_storytelling.example: 30-story building structure communicates espionage mission scope; vertical descent via elevators/stairs reinforces infiltration-and-exfiltration premise
Red doors mark document locations; visual convention communicates objective without UI; player learns to seek red doors as primary goal markers
Closed doors are potential threat sources; world-state (door opening) signals enemy emergence; teaches spatial awareness
Shootable overhead lights establish player agency over environment; darkness as optional hazard players can create or avoid
Elevators are both functional traversal tool and primary world-state controller; their presence/absence, speed, and accessibility communicate mission progress and time pressure
Visual endpoint showing extraction; car presence communicates escape objective and final safe zone

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Patrol/emerge from doors at moderate frequency; shoot at player on sight; can be killed by gunfire, karate, light drop, or elevator crush
Increased aggression; spawn rate increases; more relentless pursuit of player
Responds to player input (up/down); moves between floors; can be ridden by player; player can ride on roof
Response time to input increases (slower); player loses temporal control advantage
Can exit to basement/car upon collecting all red-door documents
Teleported back to highest incomplete floor; mission restart forced
Floor darkens; reduces visibility for all entities; can be strategically exploited by player

notes: Elevator Action uses a constraint-based world-state system: the 30-story building is a literal and narrative progress ladder. Visual communication is minimal but highly functional—red doors, enemy sprites, and architectural layout convey all necessary information. The alarm mechanic is a key world-state transition that manifests primarily through behavioral change (enemy aggression, elevator lag) rather than visual transformation. The game communicates danger through proximity (enemy spawn zones = doors) and environmental hazards (shafts, crushers). No faction identity system exists beyond player vs. enemy dichotomy; distinction is purely role-based (secret agent vs. antagonist agents). The game's espionage narrative is reinforced entirely through mechanical metaphor: vertical descent, locked doors, timed pressure, and extraction point.
